coredata.dc:title: Ultralong lifetime room-temperature phosphorescence in aqueous medium from silica confined polymer carbon dots for autoluminescence-free bioimaging and multilevel information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Chenxia
Bai, Yunfeng
Chen, Zezhong
Geng, Fanshen
Qin, Jun
Zhou, Tao
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence (URTP) materials have innate superiority in eliminating autoluminescence for bioimaging, but achieving URTP in aqueous medium is a highly challenging task, especially for carbon dot-based materials. Here we present a facile one-step hydrothermal method to effectively promote RTP emission in an aqueous medium by confining polymer carbon dots (PCDs) in silica nanospheres (SNSs) with tetraethylorthosilicate as only raw material. The polymer carbon dots-silica nanosphere composites (PCDs-SNSs) exhibit ultralong lifetime up to 2.19 s (nearly 9 s to naked eye) at ambient temperature and atmosphere. To the best of our knowledge, this is the longest RTP lifetime of carbon dot-based materials in aqueous media to date. As indicated, RTP should originate from the subfluorescent CO groups. The shielding of SNSs to all kinds of quenching in the confined space in which there are strong interactions between PCDs and SNSs, e.g, covalent bond and hydrogen bond, which enhances the rigidity of cross-linked framework and protects emitting centers from non-radiative deactive processes of triplet state, is the main reason for the URTP observed in aqueous media. Besides, the PCDs-SNSs solution exhibits outstanding stability and low cytotoxicity. Based on the distinctive properties in aqueous media, the PCDs-SNSs have been successfully used as autoluminescence-free probes for bioimaging with high sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio, and as security ink for strong concealment and moisture-related multilevel information protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer carbon dots
Aqueous medium
Room temperature phosphorescence
Bioimaging
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and mechanism of PCDs-SNSs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of PCDs-SNSs. (b) Size distribution histogram of PCDs-SNSs. (c–d) HRTEM images of PCDs-SNSs. (e) Raman spectrum of PCDs-SNSs. (f) XRD pattern of PCDs-SNSs. (g–k) Elemental maps of C, O, and Si for PCDs-SNSs.

(a) XPS spectrum of PCDs-SNSs. (b–d) High-resolution XPS spectra and fitting results of C 1s, O 1s, and Si 2p for PCDs-SNSs. (e) FTIR spectrum of PCDs-SNSs. (f) Proposed schematic diagram of PCDs-SNSs cross-linked covalently together.

(a) Normalized UV–vis absorbance (Abs), fluorescence emission (FL), and excitation spectra (FLE) of PCDs-SNSs solution. (b) Emission color coordinates of fluorescence and phosphorescence (λex = 360 nm) of PCDs-SNSs solution. (c) Normalized fluorescence (FL) and phosphorescence emission (PL) and excitation spectra (FLE/PLE) of PCDs-SNSs solution. (d) Temperature-dependent transient photoluminescent decays of PCDs-SNSs solution.

(a) 3D phosphorescence contour map of PCDs-SNSs solution. (b) Phosphorescence decay curve and fitting curve of PCDs-SNSs solution under ambient conditions (λex = 360 nm and λem = 505 nm). (c) Normalized luminescence and phosphorescence spectra of PCDs-SNSs solution at low temperature (77 K) under excitation of 360 nm.

(a) TEM image of PCDs-2, inset is high-resolution HRTEM image. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and excitation spectrum measured at 374 nm of PCDs-2 solution. (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum of PCDs-2 solution. (d) FTIR spectra of PCDs-SNSs powder and high-temperature treated PCDs-SNSs powder.
(a) Bright-field, (b) fluorescence, (c) merge and (d–f) phosphorescence images of PCDs-SNSs-treated MCF7 cells. Scale bar: 50 μm.
Data encryption and graphic security applications.
(a) Schematic representation of the synthesis process and (b) their luminescence properties of PCDs-SNSs solution.

head.ce:title: Ultralong lifetime room-temperature phosphorescence in aqueous medium from silica confined polymer carbon dots for autoluminescence-free bioimaging and multilevel information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, 037009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, 037009, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence (URTP) materials have innate superiority in eliminating autoluminescence for bioimaging, but achieving URTP in aqueous medium is a highly challenging task, especially for carbon dot-based materials. Here we present a facile one-step hydrothermal method to effectively promote RTP emission in an aqueous medium by confining polymer carbon dots (PCDs) in silica nanospheres (SNSs) with tetraethylorthosilicate as only raw material. The polymer carbon dots-silica nanosphere composites (PCDs-SNSs) exhibit ultralong lifetime up to 2.19 s (nearly 9 s to naked eye) at ambient temperature and atmosphere. To the best of our knowledge, this is the longest RTP lifetime of carbon dot-based materials in aqueous media to date. As indicated, RTP should originate from the subfluorescent CO groups. The shielding of SNSs to all kinds of quenching in the confined space in which there are strong interactions between PCDs and SNSs, e.g, covalent bond and hydrogen bond, which enhances the rigidity of cross-linked framework and protects emitting centers from non-radiative deactive processes of triplet state, is the main reason for the URTP observed in aqueous media. Besides, the PCDs-SNSs solution exhibits outstanding stability and low cytotoxicity. Based on the distinctive properties in aqueous media, the PCDs-SNSs have been successfully used as autoluminescence-free probes for bioimaging with high sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio, and as security ink for strong concealment and moisture-related multilevel information protection.

ce:section.ce:para: Imaging with optical probes plays an extremely important role in medical diagnosis [1], drug delivery [2] and image-guided surgery [3]. Fluorescence imaging, the most common bioimaging technique, has been widely used in biomedical research because of its safety, rapidity and low cost [4,5]. However, a major problem with fluorescence imaging is the inevitable tissue autoluminescence, which spans the entire visible region and may overlap with target probe emission, thus interfering with bioimaging. On the other hand, it is also very difficult to separate the probe signal from autoluminescence due to the similar nanosecond lifetime of fluorescent probes and autoluminescence [6]. Therefore, the development of new and long-lived optical probes to avoid the interference of short-lived autoluminescence is highly desirable for bioimaging. Ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence (URTP) that can emit for seconds to hours after removing the excitation light source has innate superiority in eliminating autoluminescence [7–9].

Information leakage is a growing global security challenge, and the customary security methods are not sufficient to deal with currently new and frequent high-level threats [10]. In recent years, security technologies include optical encryption, plasmonic security labels, and magnetic response have been developed to prevent encrypted information from being read or the data from being forged [11–14]. Among them, optical encryption has received increasing attention, especially URTP materials are widely applied to security label, which possess more advantages than fluorescent materials [15,16].

However, there are significant challenges in achieving URTP due to unstable triplet excitons, solvent-assisted relaxation, oxygen-induced quenching, and ineffective intersystem crossing [17–20]. Until now, phosphorescent materials are still mainly restricted to conventional metal coordination complexes and organic crystals, but both suffer from expensive raw materials, relatively complicated preparation and, in some cases, high toxicity [21–23]. These shortcomings greatly hinder their further development.

Carbon dots, typical fluorescent nanomaterials, mainly including carbon nanodots, graphene quantum dots, and polymer carbon dots (PCDs), have attracted sustained attention owing to their superior optical performance, facile preparation, low toxicity, and biocompatibility [5,24–26]. Interestingly, the carbon dot-based RTP phenomenon was discovered by dispersing carbon dots into a poly(vinyl alcohol) matrix with lifetime of 380 ms [27], which makes carbon dots promising candidates for RTP materials. Since then, RTP has also been reported by embedding carbon dots into a variety of matrices, such as potassium aluminum sulfate [28], recrystallized urea/biuret [29], NaCl hybrid crystals [30], zeolites [31] and self-protected polymer matrices [17,32,33]. Unfortunately, these materials exhibit phosphorescent properties only in the solid phase, mainly attributed to the fact that most encapsulation matrices are susceptible to interference and disruption in solution and thus fail to provide effective confined shielding, resulting in phosphorescent quenching by solvent assisted relaxation and dissolved oxygen. Alternatively, some matrices themselves lack of functional groups and have poor dispersion in solution, so agglomeration can easily occur. Recently, the incorporation of carbon dots and cyanuric acid/melamine [34,35], SiO2 [36–40] or molten salt [41] was utilized to achieve RTP in aqueous media, but they still suffer from relatively short luminescence lifetime. It is also noted that current construction strategies mainly focus on multi-step synthesis routes, thus increasing the complexity of experiments.

In this study, we reported a one-step hydrothermal method to prepare URTP polymer carbon dot-silica nanosphere composites (PCDs-SNSs) with tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) as only raw material. During hydrothermal process, PCDs were formed and simultaneously encapsulated within silica nanospheres (SNSs). Analysis shows that the generated carbonyl groups (CO) are the main luminescent centers. The confined space of SNSs in which there are strong interactions between PCDs and SNSs, e.g., covalent bond and hydrogen bond, shields non-radiative deactive processes of the triplet state and is believed to be responsible for the URTP observed in aqueous media. The luminescence lifetime of PCDs-SNSs solution is up to 2.19 s (nearly 9 s to naked eye). In light of these properties, the PCDs-SNSs have been successfully used as autoluminescence-free probes for bioimaging. In addition, the PCDs-SNSs are also explored as security ink for strong concealment and moisture-related multilevel information protection. This work not only provides a novel synthesis route, but also inspires more research into the application of carbon dot-based URTP materials in aqueous media.

Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and triethanolamine were purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co. Ltd. Ethylenediamine, ethanol, phosphoric acid and citric acid were obtained from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded with a JEOL JEM-2100F electron microscope. The particle size distribution was measured by a DelsaMax PRO Zeta potential dynamic light scattering analyzer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was acquired by a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were determined with a Rigaku SmartLab SE diffractometer. Raman spectra were performed using a Renishaw inVia Raman spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 514 nm laser. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured using a WQF520 FTIR spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis was recorded by a PerkinElmer STA 6000 simultaneous thermal analyzer at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in an air atmosphere. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer Lambda-35 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence spectra were achieved on a PerkinElmer LS-55 photoluminescence spectrophotometer. Decay curves and absolute quantum yield were measured with a HORIBA QM 8000 spectrofluorometer at room temperature. Photoluminescence spectra at 77 K and temperature-dependent transient photoluminescence decays were measured using an Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrofluorometer equipped with a microsecond flash lamp. Bioimaging was performed on an Olympus FV1200V laser scanning confocal microscope.

TEOS (0.1 mL) was dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL). After continuous stirring for 20 min, the solution was transferred to a poly(p-phenylene) reactor and heated at 240 °C for 15 h. The PCDs-SNSs solution was then obtained by dialysis (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 5 h.
The PCDs-2 solution was acquired by the same procedure as PCDs-SNSs except that TEOS was replaced by ethanol.
User friendly, the PCDs-SNSs aqueous dispersion (50 mg/mL) can be directly employed as security printing ink for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

Carbon dots with blue fluorescence emission were prepared according to the previous report [42]. Citric acid (1.051 g) and ethylenediamine (0.335 mL) were dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL), then the solution was transferred to a poly(p-phenylene) reactor and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The resultant carbon dots were freeze-dried and then redispersed in deionized water (5 mg/mL) as interference fluorescence ink for information encryption.

Carbon dots with RTP in solid states were prepared by microwave assisted method [43]. Triethanolamine (3.0 mL) was dissolved in ultrapure water (8 mL), then phosphoric acid (9.0 mL) was added drop by drop with stirring. Microwave reaction was conducted at 650 W for 3 min. After cooling, the solid sample was dissolved in ultrapure water (25 mL) and adjusted to pH 4. Subsequently, the aqueous solution of the crude product was purified through centrifugation (9000 rpm/min for 10 min) and dialysis (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 10 h. The final products were freeze-dried and then redispersed in deionized water (50 mg/mL) as interference RTP ink for information encryption.

The cytotoxicity of PCDs-SNSs was assessed by the standard MTT assay with human breast cancer (MCF7) cells. First, MCF7 cells were seeded into the wells of a 96-well plate (5000 cells per well) and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Subsequently, the PCDs-SNSs solution with different concentrations (0, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 130 μg/mL) were introduced to each well and incubated for another 24 h. After that, 10 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was pipetted into co-culture with cells for 4 h, after removing the cell culture medium, 150 μL of DMSO was added to each well. Finally, the absorbance of each solution was recorded at 570 nm.

For cell imaging, MCF7 cells were seeded into confocal dishes (1.5 × 105 cells per milliliter) and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Then, 10 μL of PCDs-SNSs solution (80 μg/mL) was introduced to co-culture with cells for 12 h, and the cells were washed three times with PBS before bioimaging.

The overall process of synthesizing PCDs-SNSs by a one-step hydrothermal treatment is illustrated in Scheme 1 a. At the initial reaction stage, TEOS was hydrolyzed to produce silicic acid and ethanol, the latter was followed by further cross-linking and carbonization to form PCDs. While for silicic acid, it was polycondensed to generate silica spheres. During this process, the resulting PCDs were encapsulated within silica spheres, and finally PCDs-SNSs were formed.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were used to reveal the morphology and size of PCDs-SNSs. In the TEM image (Fig. 1 a), as-prepared PCDs-SNSs show uniform spherical shapes with particle size of about 25–60 nm. The histogram of size distribution measured by DLS (Fig. 1b) displays that the average size of sphere is approximately 46.6 nm in agreement with the TEM result. It is critical to obtain stable dispersion with small particles. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1c) provides a clear zoom-in profile of PCDs-SNSs, and it can be seen that the PCDs are well dispersed inside the silicon sphere. As well, no obvious lattice fringes are found in the sample (Fig. 1d), indicating the amorphous feature of PCDs and SNSs [26,44,45]. This can also be confirmed by Raman data (Fig. 1e) that no typical Raman peaks are detected. Furthermore, powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern exhibits a wide diffraction peak centered at 21.9° (Fig. 1f), which signifies partial order characteristic of polymer carbon dots [26,32]. In addition, elemental mapping images (Fig. 1g–k) definitely suggest that PCDs-SNSs are composed of C, O, and Si elements, in which the carbon from PCDs is evenly distributed in the interior of SNSs, indicating the successful confinement of PCDs.

To further investigate the chemical bond effect between PCDs and SNSs, we carried out X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements. The XPS spectrum shows that PCDs-SNSs consist of C, O and Si elements with compositions found to be 44.39%, 36.00% and 19.61%, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The deconvolution of the high-resolution C 1s band (Fig. 2b) demonstrates three peaks at 284.6, 286.7 and 288.0 eV, corresponding to CC/C–C, C–O and CO groups, respectively [31]. The O 1s band (Fig. 2c) is fitted into three peaks at 532.0, 533.3 and 535.1 eV, indicating the presence of CO, Si–O and C–O bonds [46,47]. The Si 2p band (Fig. 2d) involves two peaks including Si–O–C (102.7 eV) and Si−Ox (105.1 eV), which indicates the presence of covalent bonds between PCDs and SNSs [37]. By comparison in FTIR analysis (Fig. 2e), the same functional groups of CO, Si–O–C and Si–O are observed at 1601, 1211 and 476 cm−1, respectively [48–50]. Besides, silica is manifest by Si–O–Si asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibration at 1103 and 798 cm−1 [49,50]. Additional, the broad and distinct absorption peak near 3406 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H group for hydrogen bonding [47]. Based on the above results, we propose that silica and partial PCDs have been cross-linked through Si–O–C bonding and hydrogen bonding. The structure and possible bonding were illustrated in Fig. 2f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and mechanism of PCDs-SNSs
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, the optical properties of PCDs-SNSs solution were studied. When illuminated with 365 nm UV lamp, the PCDs-SNSs solution exhibits a blue color, corresponding to maximum emission peak at 434 nm (Fig. 3 a). Here, UV–vis absorption spectrum displays an obvious absorption peak at 305 nm, which is attributed to the n–π* transition of CO [27,51]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence excitation spectrum covers a broad spectrum range from 250 to 450 nm with an optimal excitation at 364 nm, which overlaps the absorption band of CO, implying that CO bonds are primarily responsible for fluorescence emission [26,27,51]. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission spectra under different excitations were shown in Fig. S1. The distinctive excitation-dependent behavior from PCDs is observed. Fluorescence decay curve exhibits bi-exponential decay with an average lifetime of 6.84 ns (Fig. S2 and Table S1). These photoluminescence characteristics reflect the existence of multiple emitting centers in the PCDs-SNSs, similar to many previous reports [35,52]. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield is measured to be 10.5% under the excitation of 360 nm.

After turning off the UV lamp, an ultralong green afterglow (lasting for nearly 9 s to naked eye) is observed under ambient conditions (Scheme 1b and Movie S1. The chromaticity diagram is displayed in Fig. 3b. The afterglow chromaticity coordinates of PCDs-SNSs solution are (0.242, 0.534), close to the green color, and the fluorescence chromaticity coordinates are (0.156, 0.125), close to the blue region. From the afterglow emission spectrum, we can see an obvious wavelength red-shift compared to fluorescence emission spectrum with an excitation of 360 nm (Fig. 3c), implying the afterglow nature of phosphorescence rather than delayed fluorescence [53]. To make further clarification, the temperature-dependent transient photoluminescent decays were measured (Fig. 3d and Table S2). The photoluminescent lifetime decreased gradually as the temperature went up from 78 to 298 K, implying the cryogenic temperature effect for the restriction of intermolecular motions to reduce nonradiative relaxation rate. This phenomenon serves to confirm the triplet feature of phosphorescence again [54,55].

To obtain insights of URTP characteristics of the PCDs-SNSs solution, a further set of experiments were performed. 3D phosphorescence contour map (Fig. 4 a) displays a narrow emission region with maximum emission center located at 505 nm under 360 nm excitation. As the excitation wavelength changes from 280 nm to 400 nm, the URTP emission wavelength shows a slight shift (Fig. S3). In addition, similar excitation spectra for fluorescence and URTP in conjunction with nearly identical optimal excitation wavelength at about 360 nm are observed (Fig. 3c), indicating that the fluorescence and URTP emission stem from the same chemical structure [55]. According to the result of XPS and FTIR analysis, RTP should originate from subfluorescent CO groups because they have strong spin-orbit coupling, which facilitates the generation of triplet excitons through intersystem crossing, in agreement with previously reported results [34,51,56]. Then, RTP decay curve and corresponding fitting curve were shown in Fig. 4b. PCDs-SNSs exhibit triexponential decay pattern with three lifetimes of 1.83 s (62.25%), 3.24 s (18.78%) and 1.09 s (18.97%) (Table S3). These multiple lifetimes imply the existence of diverse decay channels that are caused by various chemical environments of CO groups in PCDs-SNSs [26,27]. In particular, the average lifetime of URTP is calculated to be 2.19 s. To the best of our knowledge, this is the longest RTP lifetime of carbon dot-based materials in aqueous media to date (Table S4). Furthermore, the energy gap between lowest triplet and singlet states can be evaluated as 0.38 eV (S1 = 2.86 eV and T1 = 2.48 eV) based on the low-temperature luminescence spectrum at 77 K (blue, where the peak at 433 nm is the fluorescence peak and the interstitial peak at 500 nm is the phosphorescence peak) and the phosphorescence spectrum (green) (Fig. 4c). This small energy gap is favorable for effective intersystem crossing to populate triplet excitons [52]. The absolute RTP quantum yield is measured to be 7.4% at ambient conditions under the excitation of 360 nm. At last, the stability of PCDs-SNSs solution was explored. RTP intensity showed nearly identical optical curves after three months’ storage, or continuous excitation of 365 nm for 60 min (Figs. S4–S5). The latter facts provide clear evidence that the PCDs-SNSs solution exhibits outstanding stability and is suitable for practical application.

On the basis of above results we conclude the possible photoluminescence mechanism of PCDs-SNSs in aqueous solution and illustrate as follows. During the formation of PCDs from ethanol with oxidation, crosslink polymerization and dehydration reactions, the subfluorescent groups (CO) are generated and act as luminescent centers. Served as a protecting matrix, the SNSs can lock and separate subfluorescent CO groups in PCDs, which effectively stabilizes the triplet state by restraining nonradiative transition and preventing oxygen quenching in aqueous media. Besides, Si–O–C bonds and hydrogen bonds between PCDs and SNSs further enhance the rigidity of cross-linked framework and protect emitting centers from attacks, which jointly promote URTP emission. To further verify the proposed mechanism, PCDs-2 were synthesized by replacing TEOS with ethanol under otherwise identical conditions. The morphology of PCDs-2 was investigated by TEM to verify the generation of nanoparticles. The TEM image of PCDs-2 shows uniform distribution with a particle size of about 5 nm (Fig. 5 a), consistent with the PCDs in PCDs-SNSs. Furthermore, no obvious lattice fringes are observed in most of the nanoparticles by high-resolution TEM image, indicating the nature of polymer carbon dots. Compared to the PCDs-SNSs solution, the PCDs-2 solution exhibits similar fluorescence emission but no RTP (Fig. 5b), indicating that the confined shielding of SNSs is indispensable for RTP. Fig. 5b also shows that PCDs-2 solution has characteristic excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. Besides, fluorescence excitation spectrum of PCDs-2 solution displays a broad band from 220 to 350 nm with the maximum excitation peak at 290 nm, highly overlapping with the UV–vis absorption of CO bonds (Fig. 5c). These data suggest that the photoluminescence of PCDs-SNSs relies on CO functional groups of PCDs from ethanol. In addition, another control experiment was designed by high-temperature treatment of PCDs-SNSs at 800 °C for 10 h. As expected, no luminescence phenomenon is observed from the treated PCDs-SNSs, which is mainly attributed to the loss of CO bonds as a result of high-temperature treatment. FTIR characterization was employed to validate this conclusion (Fig. 5d). On the other hand, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was implemented to further verify the change of PCDs-SNSs (Fig. S6). TGA shows a mass loss of 2.6% in the temperature range of 30–170 °C, which is attributed to the evaporation of surface adsorbent water and crystalline water. The weight loss of around 7.4% appears beyond 170 °C and is associated with the decomposition of carbonyl and hydroxyl. The above experimental results reconfirm our speculated mechanism.

To reveal the bioimaging potential, the cytotoxicity of PCDs-SNSs was first evaluated by the standard MTT assay using MCF7 cells. Fig. S7 shows that the cellular viability was over 90% even at concentrations of PCDs-SNSs up to 130 μg/mL, indicating that PCDs-SNSs have good biocompatibility and relatively low cytotoxicity. In addition, bioimaging of PCDs-SNSs in MCF7 cells was investigated. From the confocal fluorescence and bright-field images (Fig. 6 a and b), we can see that MCF7 Cells show blue fluorescence emission at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm, indicating that PCDs-SNSs can cross the cell membranes with good permeability. After turning off the excitation source, the phosphorescence images can be clearly captured by collecting sequential images (Fig. 6c–f). The successful phosphorescence imaging of PCDs-SNSs solution highlights the advantage of URTP in bioimaging, which prevents autoluminescence interference caused by excitation light and exhibits high sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio.

Given the outstanding feature, PCDs-SNSs solution was employed as advanced security ink for confidential information protection (Fig. 7 ). A designed pattern “88888” as well as a logo of tulip flower were printed with different inks on nonfluorescent filter paper. Here, PCDs-SNSs solution was used to label the black part, the blue and green sections were coated with interference ink A and ink B, respectively. Both interference inks have similar fluorescence property as that of PCDs-SNSs solution, and ink B also has green RTP characteristic in dry condition. In practice, the printed patterns are invisible after natural drying, which is crucial for security protection. As to the digital pattern, when a 365 nm UV lamp was turned on, the pattern showed the blue “88888”, serving as the first layer of security code. Once UV lamp off, the pattern turned green and “60237” appeared, thus offering the second layer of security. Significantly, the correct code “57231” was visualized only after spraying with water. Similarly, the tulip flower pattern emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp. When switching off the excitation, the petals and stem parts exhibited green but the leaves were invisible. After wetting, only the petals were captured by naked eye. To ensure reliable performance of information encryption, the RTP intensity variation of filter paper (e.g., graphic security) was measured by five dry and wet cycles (Fig. S8). The RTP intensity of PCDs-SNSs printed on paper showed a slight change (<10%), indicating that PCDs-SNSs ink has good rereading performance in information encryption. Apparently, the multilevel information encryption contributes to improve the encryption ability and prevent valuable information from being read or the data from being forged.

In summary, PCDs uniformly encapsulated in the Si–O framework of silica spheres have been fabricated through one-step hydrothermal treatment of TEOS without any other addition. In this manner, the PCDs-SNSs with average dimensions of 46.6 nm achieve URTP lifetime of 2.19 s (nearly 9 s to naked eye) in aqueous media, and keep stable for months. Analysis reveals that the photoluminescence of PCDs-SNSs relies on CO groups of PCDs from ethanol. Moreover, the confined shielding of silica matrix together with enhanced cross-linking effect by Si–O–C bonds and hydrogen bonds effectively stabilize triplet excitons by restraining the nonradiative transition and preventing the oxygen quenching, consequently triggering RTP. The PCDs-SNSs have been successfully used for autoluminescence-free bioimaging with high sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio, and as advanced security ink for the protection of confidential information.

Chenxia Hao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yunfeng Bai: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Zezhong Chen: Investigation. Fanshen Geng: Investigation. Jun Qin: Investigation. Tao Zhou: Investigation, Validation. Feng Feng: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2021L368), Cultivate Scientific Research Excellence Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2020KJ023) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2020-133).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109890.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular afterglow imaging with bright, biodegradable polymer nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Miao Q, Xie C, Zhen X, Lyu Y, Duan H, Liu X, et al. Molecular afterglow imaging with bright, biodegradable polymer nanoparticles. Nat Biotechnol. 2017;35(11):1102-1110.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Real-time imaging of oxidative and nitrosative stress in the liver of live animals for drug-toxicity testing

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advanced nanotechnology leading the way to multimodal imaging‐guided precision surgical therapy
